steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage says Harper's speaker criticizes activists who champion causes in other countries while overlooking local concerns. The correct quotation must show both halves of that contrast: attention to distant/foreign suffering and neglect of nearby/home suffering.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘"Men may tread down the poor and lowly, / May crush them in anger and hate, / But surely the mills of God's justice / Will grind out the grist of their fate." (from "An Appeal to My Countrywomen")’: describes divine justice grinding out the fate of oppressors. No foreign-versus-domestic contrast. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘"When ye plead for the wrecked and fallen, / The exile from far-distant shores, / Remember that men are still wasting / Life's crimson around your own doors." (from "An Appeal to My Countrywomen")’: 'far-distant shores' names the foreign focus; 'your own doors' names the local suffering being ignored. The demand to 'remember' home-front tragedy directly embodies Harper's criticism. ✓
  • Choice C — ‘"God bless our native land, / Land of the newly free, / Oh may she ever stand / For truth and liberty." (from "God Bless Our Native Land")’: a patriotic blessing with no contrast between foreign and domestic causes. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘"Let me make the songs for the people, / Songs for the old and young; / Songs to stir like a battle cry / Wherever they are sung." (from "Songs for the People")’: describes the power of song, with no reference to foreign versus local concerns. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
